title: Trauma and the Body's Capacity: Building Tolerance for Sensation and Emotion
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the body’s capacity for sensation and emotion and learn science-backed ways to safely build tolerance and resilience.
body:
Understanding Trauma Beyond the Mind

Trauma isn’t just a psychological event stored in memory; it’s a deeply embodied experience that rewires how your body perceives and handles sensation and emotion. As Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, emphasizes in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma lodges itself in the body, often triggering physiological reactions long after the event has passed.1 For many who have tried traditional therapy yet remain stuck, this disconnect between mind and body is a key piece of the puzzle.

What Does ‘Capacity’ Mean in the Context of Trauma?

When we talk about the body’s capacity, we’re referring to its ability to tolerate and process internal sensations and emotions without becoming overwhelmed. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, describes capacity as the threshold between feeling safe enough in the body to notice sensation and emotion without triggering a dysregulated response.2 Trauma reduces this threshold, shrinking the window of tolerance, which is the optimal zone where a person can experience stress or emotion without shutting down or flooding.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory sheds light on this by explaining how the autonomic nervous system (ANS) shifts into states of defense when it perceives threat—even if the threat is a flashback or emotional trigger.3 When stuck in fight, flight, or freeze, the body’s capacity to engage with sensation and emotion safely narrows.

Why Building Capacity Matters

Expanding capacity is crucial because it allows for integration of traumatic memories and emotions in a way that feels manageable. Without this, attempts to process trauma can lead to retraumatization or emotional overwhelm.

"Healing trauma isn’t about pushing through pain but learning to meet sensation and emotion with enough safety to tolerate their presence." – Deb Dana4

Increasing capacity helps restore nervous system regulation, giving you more control over your reactions and improving resilience. It’s not about erasing trauma but making space for it to exist without hijacking your nervous system.

How Trauma Shrinks Capacity: The Neurobiology

Early trauma or overwhelming stress can impair the brain’s ability to regulate autonomic responses. Pat Ogden, a leader in somatic psychology, notes that trauma disrupts the integration between the brain’s emotional centers (like the amygdala) and higher cortical areas responsible for regulation.5 This disconnect results in hyperarousal, hypoarousal, or dissociation—states that reflect a compromised capacity for sensation and emotion.

Moreover, trauma affects interoception—the brain’s ability to sense internal bodily states. Research shows that impaired interoception is common in trauma survivors, leading to difficulties identifying and naming emotions, often called alexithymia.6 This adds another layer of challenge to building capacity: it’s hard to tolerate what you can’t recognize.

Science-Backed Practices to Build Capacity

Fortunately, research-backed somatic approaches offer ways to gradually expand the window of tolerance and increase capacity. Here are key methods supported by leading experts:

  • Mindful Body Awareness: Dan Siegel highlights the importance of mindfulness practices that cultivate noticing without judgment. This trains the brain to hold sensation and emotion with less reactivity.7
  • Somatic Experiencing: Peter Levine’s approach uses titration—exposing the nervous system to small doses of distressing sensation—to prevent overwhelm and build tolerance.2
  • Polyvagal-Informed Regulation: Deb Dana’s work focuses on tracking autonomic states and shifting between them using breath, movement, and social engagement cues.4
  • Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Pat Ogden combines mindfulness, movement, and talk therapy to restore bodily regulation and improve capacity.5
  • EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing): Francine Shapiro introduced EMDR, which helps reprocess trauma memories safely by engaging bilateral stimulation and reducing emotional distress.8

Practical Steps to Expand Your Capacity for Sensation and Emotion

Building capacity isn’t about rushing or forcing exposure to trauma-related sensations. It’s a gradual, patient process that respects your nervous system’s current limits. Here are actionable strategies:

  • Start with Grounding: Engage your senses in the present moment. Notice what you see, hear, feel physically—this builds safety.
  • Practice Gentle Tracking: Pay attention to subtle sensations without trying to change them. Label what you notice (“tightness,” “warmth,” “tingling”).
  • Use Breath Regulation: Slow, deep, rhythmic breathing signals safety to the vagus nerve and calms hyperarousal.
  • Engage in Movement: Gentle, mindful movements like yoga or walking reconnect you to your body and release stuck energy.
  • Establish Safe Relationships: Social engagement cues activate the parasympathetic nervous system, increasing safety and capacity.3
  • Seek Professional Guidance: Work with somatically informed therapists who understand capacity building and can guide titration safely.

The Role of Compassion and Patience

Gabor Maté reminds us that trauma healing is non-linear and requires self-compassion. The body’s capacity may fluctuate day-to-day depending on stress levels, sleep, and environment.9 Frustration or self-judgment only exacerbate nervous system dysregulation. Embracing a compassionate stance toward your experience fosters safety, which is the foundation of capacity expansion.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Your Body’s Wisdom

Trauma may have shrunk your body’s capacity to tolerate sensation and emotion, but it is not permanent. With science-backed somatic practices, you can gently rebuild this capacity, restoring regulation and resilience. The body, as van der Kolk asserts, holds the key to healing when approached with respect, patience, and informed care.1

Remember, building capacity is not about erasing pain but learning to be with it safely, so that sensation and emotion become sources of insight and vitality rather than overwhelm.

References

  1. van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.
  2. Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  3. Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Co.
  4. Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Co.
  5. Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Co.
  6. Seth, A. K. (2013). Interoceptive inference, emotion, and the embodied self. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 17(11), 565-573.
  7. Siegel, D. J. (2010). The Mindful Therapist: A Clinician's Guide to Mindsight and Neural Integration. W.W. Norton & Co.
  8. Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  9. Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.

faqs.q: What is meant by "capacity" in trauma healing?
faqs.a: Capacity refers to the body and mind’s ability to tolerate and process sensations and emotions without becoming overwhelmed or dysregulated.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the nervous system?
faqs.a: Trauma disrupts autonomic nervous system regulation, often causing hyperarousal, hypoarousal, or dissociation, which narrows the window of tolerance for sensation and emotion.

faqs.q: Can somatic practices help if therapy alone hasn’t worked?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic approaches address trauma stored in the body and can build nervous system regulation and capacity, complementing talk therapy or serving as an alternative path.

faqs.q: What are some safe ways to build tolerance for difficult emotions?
faqs.a: Gradual exposure through mindful body awareness, breath regulation, movement, grounding techniques, and working with a somatically informed therapist are effective and safe strategies.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and offering science-based healing approaches.

affiliateProducts.title: In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Introduces Somatic Experiencing and practical tools for releasing trauma through the body.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Explains how understanding the autonomic nervous system can transform trauma therapy and self-regulation.

metaTitle: Trauma & Body Capacity: Building Tolerance for Emotion
metaDescription: Learn how trauma impacts your body’s capacity for sensation and emotion, and discover science-backed ways to build tolerance and resilience.
author: The Oracle Lover
